# **Relightable Gaussian Splatting for Foliage: Navigating Normal Ambiguity and Thin-Geometry Shading**

The evolution of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) from a static, view-dependent volume representation into a fully relightable, physically-based rendering (PBR) pipeline introduces severe geometric and radiometric challenges. This is particularly acute for thin, non-manifold structures such as foliage and vegetation. A fundamental bottleneck in deploying relightable foliage splats is the front/back normal sign ambiguity. Because raw 3DGS point clouds lack topological connectivity and explicit face winding, the nearest-neighbor normal orientation exhibits inherent sign-opposition. In dense, intersecting canopies, this sign-opposition establishes a failure floor of approximately 30%. Under traditional single-sided shading models—which strictly evaluate irradiance via the clamped dot product of the normal and the light vector—these random signs manifest as catastrophic, patchy fake shadows.  
This report provides an exhaustive investigation into the normal ambiguity problem, tailored specifically to inform architectural decisions and transmission specifications for custom compute shading pipelines. The analysis systematically evaluates normal orientation techniques deployed in state-of-the-art 3DGS inverse rendering (IR) systems, audits two-sided foliage shading practices in industry-standard real-time engines, and constructs a sign-agnostic runtime shading formulation. Furthermore, the report assesses the theoretical limitations of global sign-consistency algorithms on vegetation point clouds and proposes covariance-based normal-confidence metrics to dynamically gate shading models at runtime.

## **1\. Normal Orientation and Sign Handling in 3DGS Inverse Rendering Systems**

The challenge of extracting, orienting, and stabilizing normals within discrete Gaussian primitives has driven a rapid bifurcation in inverse rendering methodologies published between 2023 and 2026\. Because vanilla 3DGS bakes illumination entirely into view-dependent spherical harmonic (SH) coefficients without geometric constraints, normals must be artificially derived and regularized to facilitate PBR material decomposition. The literature reveals that contemporary systems address normal sign through three primary mechanisms: view-dependent axis flipping, depth-derived pseudo-normals, and continuous manifold distillation.

### **1.1 View-Dependent Flipping and Shortest-Axis Formulations**

The most prevalent approach in 3DGS inverse rendering defines the normal vector as aligned with the shortest axis of the Gaussian ellipsoid, subsequently applying artificial constraints to orient it relative to the viewing camera.  
The Geometry-Informed Relightable (GIR) framework explicitly computes the normal of each 3D Gaussian using the shortest eigenvector1. To resolve the sign ambiguity without relying on external ground-truth supervision, GIR employs a view-dependent heuristic termed a "directional masking scheme." The exact mechanism is defined as "an efficient self-regularization method that ensures the shortest axis of each visible 3D Gaussian forms an obtuse angle with the camera's principal axis"1. By mathematically forcing the dot product of the normal and the view vector to remain negative, the normal is continually flipped to face the rendering camera, preventing backface culling and ensuring the splat receives illumination.  
GaussianShader acknowledges the inherent difficulty in deriving locally-continuous surface normals from discrete spheres4. The system addresses this by introducing a representation "based on the shortest axis direction of a Gaussian sphere and learns a normal residual on this axis direction"5. While this allows the normal to deviate slightly from the strict geometric shortest axis, the base orientation remains tied to the ellipsoid's geometry, which is highly unstable for nearly isotropic (spherical) splats.  
Inter-Reflective Gaussian Splatting (IRGS) shifts the paradigm by utilizing 2D Gaussian Ray Tracing7. By flattening the primitives into 2D surfels, the normal is strictly defined as the Z-axis of the 2D disk, eliminating the shortest-axis ambiguity present in 3D ellipsoids. In its native state, IRGS utilizes view-dependent normal flipping. However, the instability of this approach under complex global illumination is highlighted by derivative works. The Radiometrically Consistent Gaussian Surfels (RadioGS) framework, built upon IRGS, explicitly states: "We disabled the view-dependent normal-flipping mechanism in IRGS. This ensures that our surfel normals remain fixed and consistent, which is essential for the stable optimization of our radiometric consistency loss"8.

### **1.2 Depth-Derived Pseudo-Normals and SDF Distillation**

To bypass the temporal instability of view-dependent flipping, several frameworks couple the 3DGS representation with rendered depth gradients or continuous Signed Distance Fields (SDFs) to enforce a unified global orientation.  
The GS-IR framework observes that the adaptive density control in 3DGS leads to loose, unconstrained geometry9. To correct this, GS-IR proposes "an efficient optimization scheme incorporating a depth-derivation-based regularization for normal estimation"10. This technique renders a depth map of the scene, calculates the analytical gradients of that depth map to form a screen-space pseudo-normal, and applies a loss function to align the individual Gaussian normals with this continuous surface proxy.  
The DeferredGS framework completely decouples the normal from the Gaussian covariance matrix. It defines additional, independent attributes on the Gaussians, where "the normal is distilled from a jointly trained signed distance function (SDF) onto the Gaussians"13. By training an implicit SDF alongside the explicit Gaussians, DeferredGS enforces a strict inside/outside volumetric boundary, allowing the normals to inherit a globally consistent orientation derived from the continuous zero-level set of the SDF.

### **1.3 External Priors and Explicit Parameters**

More recent frameworks abandon geometric derivation entirely, opting instead for external neural priors or explicit, unconstrained optimization.  
The GS-ID (Illumination Decomposition) pipeline reduces light-geometry-material ambiguity by initializing the geometry with foundation models. The exact mechanism involves reconstructing "a coarse 3DGS scene using normal priors from a diffusion model" (such as Omnidata), which injects learned, globally consistent normals into the point cloud before inverse rendering begins15.  
Relightable 3D Gaussians (R3DG) treats the normal as a completely independent, explicitly optimized parameter. The mechanism is defined such that each point is "additionally associated with a normal direction, BRDF parameters, and incident lights... divided into global and local components"18. These properties are supervised through bounding volume hierarchy (BVH) ray-tracing constraints and multi-view photometric consistency rather than relying on the physical shape of the splat19.

| Framework | Primary Normal Derivation | Sign Resolution Mechanism | Geometric Dependency |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **GIR** | Shortest Eigenvector | Directional masking (obtuse angle to camera) | High (Requires anisotropic splats) |
| **GaussianShader** | Shortest Axis \+ Residual | Normal-geometry consistency loss | High (Residual bounds) |
| **IRGS** | 2D Surfel Z-Axis | View-dependent flipping (disabled in derivatives) | Absolute (2D geometry) |
| **GS-IR** | Explicit Vector | Depth-derived pseudo-normal regularization | None (Screen-space driven) |
| **DeferredGS** | Explicit Vector | Distilled from jointly trained SDF | Volumetric (Implicit field) |
| **GS-ID** | Explicit Vector | Diffusion model priors (Omnidata) | None (Data-driven) |
| **R3DG** | Explicit Vector | Multi-view photometric consistency | None (Independent variable) |

### **1.4 Synthesis and Implications for Foliage**

The mechanisms documented in the inverse rendering literature are fundamentally hostile to the rendering of thin, highly intersecting vegetation.  
SDF distillation (as seen in DeferredGS) fails catastrophically because foliage is a non-manifold topology; a leaf has no defined "inside" or "outside" volume, rendering the implicit distance field ill-posed20. Depth-derived pseudo-normals (as used in GS-IR and GaussianShader) fail because the depth map of a tree canopy is highly discontinuous. The depth gradients at the overlapping edges of millions of leaves produce extreme, high-frequency noise that destroys normal alignment. Finally, the camera-facing regularization utilized by GIR is viable for opaque, solid surfaces but breaks instantly for foliage under arbitrary, dynamic lighting. If a leaf normal is always dynamically flipped to face the rendering camera, a static light source positioned behind the leaf will suddenly act as a direct front-light the moment the camera's trajectory crosses the light vector. This destroys temporal illumination stability and renders accurate transmission impossible. Therefore, the 3DGS runtime for foliage cannot rely on the orientation mechanisms standardized in current inverse rendering literature.

## **2\. Two-Sided and Foliage Shading Practices in Real-Time Engines**

To establish a physically plausible baseline for runtime rendering that circumvents the limitations of current 3DGS frameworks, an examination of how industry-standard game engines handle two-sided shading is required. These engines have long dealt with the complexities of rendering thin, non-manifold geometry, developing specialized workarounds for normal ambiguity, energy conservation, and transmission.

### **2.1 Unreal Engine: Topological Reliance and the Subsurface Model**

Unreal Engine implements a dedicated "Two Sided Foliage" shading model specifically engineered for thin surfaces21. This model fundamentally relies on polygon topology to resolve shading orientations.  
When the "Two Sided" property is enabled on a material, the engine's rasterizer evaluates the face winding of the polygons. If a backface is detected relative to the camera, the engine explicitly flips the normal (![][image1]) so that standard BRDF lighting calculations function correctly for the backside of the mesh21. To handle transmission, the model utilizes a Subsurface Color input. This input acts as an attenuation multiplier for light passing through the surface. Unreal computes indirect subsurface scattering by "sampling the environment cubemap in the direction, opposite of the normal"23.  
However, this reliance on automatic normal flipping introduces a severe dilemma known in technical art pipelines as the "edited normal" problem. A standard practice for rendering grass or ground clutter is to edit the vertex normals of the foliage cards to point straight up along the Z-axis to ensure seamless lighting blending with the underlying terrain23. When custom normals are utilized, Unreal's automatic backface flipping produces incorrect, inverted shading vectors. To counteract this, developers must manually inject a TwoSidedSign node into the High-Level Shading Language (HLSL) material graph, multiplying the authored normal map by this sign to restore consistency25.

### **2.2 SpeedTree: Geometric Fudging and Angular Thresholds**

SpeedTree, the dominant procedural vegetation middleware, combats normal ambiguity through heavy geometric pre-processing and angular thresholds before the asset is evaluated by the engine's shader.  
To prevent hard shading seams along branch and leaf silhouettes, SpeedTree utilizes a view-dependent vertex interpolation scheme. The algorithm monitors the relationship between the normal and the camera: "If the dot product of the vertex normal and the view vector changes sign along a triangle edge... we find a new vertex... where the dot product of the interpolated view vector and interpolated normal equals zero"27.  
For the back-lighting of leaves, SpeedTree calculates an angle, ![][image2], between the light direction and the view vector. If ![][image2] indicates the camera is looking directly toward the primary light source through the canopy, the shader interpolates the base leaf color toward a pre-calculated brightened, yellow-tinted vector to simulate transmission27. Furthermore, the software allows artists to explicitly toggle "flip backside normals" off26. When disabled, the software often pushes the normals outward spherically from the tree's center of mass (a process called "puffing"). This deliberately destroys the local, physically accurate leaf normal to create a unified, cloud-like ambient lighting response across the entire canopy25.

### **2.3 Frostbite: Wrapped Lighting and Phase-Driven Subsurface**

The Frostbite engine's approach to rendering thick foliage and fast translucency heavily relies on bypassing strict normal-dependency for the transmitted light lobe, treating transmission more like a volumetric phase function than a surface reflection.  
In architectural presentations regarding Frostbite's transition to PBR, it was detailed that the engine utilizes a highly optimized subsurface scattering approximation for foliage28. Instead of relying entirely on the clamped dot product ![][image3] for transmission, the system utilizes ![][image4] multiplied by light position visibility, heavily weighted by ![][image5] (the dot product of the eye vector and light vector)29. This essentially treats the transmission of light as a forward-scattering phase function independent of the micro-variations in the surface normal.  
For advanced translucency, Frostbite implements Bidirectional Transmittance Distribution Functions (BTDFs) governed by a wavelength-varying transmission coefficient and a specific collimation factor ![][image6]. This collimation factor describes how light diffuses as it passes through the medium, where zero represents a dense, highly scattering medium, and one represents optically homogeneous glass30.

### **2.4 Energy Plausibility and Failure Modes (The "Glowing Bark" Problem)**

Two-sided shading models maintain energy plausibility by ensuring that the sum of the reflected diffuse energy, the reflected specular energy, and the transmitted subsurface energy does not exceed the total incident light. In engine implementations like Unreal's Two Sided Foliage, this is controlled via the physical thickness of the asset, ensuring that the Subsurface Color parameter only allows a fractional percentage of light to pass through.  
The primary failure mode of two-sided shading occurs when the model is inadvertently applied to thick, opaque geometry—such as tree trunks or hard, side-lit bark. If a two-sided transmission model is evaluated on a cylindrical branch, a light source positioned behind the branch will cause the front-facing bark to glow unnaturally, violating physical occlusion. In traditional engines, this is solved by masking: routing a thickness map or a branch-isolation mask into the opacity or subsurface input to clamp transmission to zero on thick geometry23. In a 3DGS pipeline where materials are recovered via inverse rendering, failure to explicitly recover a highly accurate transmission mask will result in branches glowing under backlit conditions.

## **3\. Formulations for Sign-Agnostic Runtime Shading**

The core issue facing the relightable 3DGS foliage renderer is that engines like Unreal and Frostbite rely on polygon topology (face winding) to determine front/back and compute the TwoSidedSign variable. Because Gaussian splats are disconnected primitives, face winding cannot be computed, and the \~30% sign-opposition floor in the raw data cannot be resolved at runtime.  
If a traditional PBR lighting model governed by ![][image7] is enforced, the 30% of splats with inverted normals will evaluate to zero, rendering as pure black (fake shadows) from any given angle. To resolve this, a robust mathematical framework must be established that yields the exact same radiometric energy regardless of whether the normal is evaluated as ![][image8] or ![][image9].

### **3.1 Sign-Agnostic Diffuse Lobe**

Traditional Lambertian reflectance evaluates the diffuse response based on the projected area of the surface exposed to the light:  
![][image10]  
To achieve sign-agnosticism, the evaluation must be rendered symmetrical. The physical justification for this symmetry is that a highly translucent, thin leaf acts as a micro-slab; the cross-sectional area exposed to the incident light source is identical from either side, dictated by the absolute cosine of the angle.  
The proposed diffuse formulation removes the zero-clamp and applies an absolute value:  
![][image11]  
Under this model, the front and back of the splat are illuminated identically. This completely masks the 30% normal inversion error by treating every primitive as a perfectly two-sided receiver.

### **3.2 Sign-Agnostic Specular Lobe (Two-Sided Glossy)**

For wet or waxy leaves, a specular response is required. A standard microfacet BRDF utilizes the half-vector ![][image12] and evaluates the Normal Distribution Function (NDF) via ![][image13]. If ![][image8] is inverted, ![][image13] becomes negative, and the NDF evaluates to zero, destroying the specular highlight.  
To maintain sign-agnosticism, the specular NDF must also utilize the absolute dot product:  
![][image14]  
This mathematically models a "two-sided glossy" material. A specular highlight will appear on the front of the leaf, and an identical highlight will appear if the leaf is viewed and illuminated from behind. For thin, smooth leaves covered in cuticular wax on both the adaxial and abaxial surfaces, this two-sided specular response is physically plausible and visually pleasing.

### **3.3 Backlit / Transmission Lobe Compatible with ![][image15]**

The M3 transmission specification demands a formulation for light passing through the foliage toward the camera. The initially planned term—trans \* pow(max(dot(-N,L),0)\*0.5+0.5, wrap\_power)—relies explicitly on ![][image9] to isolate the back of the leaf. However, under an absolute dot product shading regime (![][image15]), the geometric orientation is collapsed; ![][image9] and ![][image8] are completely indistinguishable.  
To solve this, the transmission lobe must be entirely decoupled from the normal ![][image8] and bound instead to the angular relationship between the View vector (![][image16]) and the Light vector (![][image17]). As demonstrated by Frostbite's architecture, forward scattering in thin media is highly dependent on the ![][image18] phase function29.  
The total geometric light intercepted by the splat is ![][image15]. The intensity of that light transmitting through the medium to the camera peaks when the light is directly behind the splat relative to the viewer, i.e., when ![][image19] (assuming ![][image16] and ![][image17] point outward from the surface).  
**Recommended Sign-Agnostic Transmission Formula:**  
**![][image20]**  
**Mechanics of the Formula:**

1. **Geometric Attenuation (![][image15]):** This term dictates the physical amount of light hitting the surface area of the splat. Because it utilizes an absolute value, it correctly attenuates edge-on leaves to zero and is entirely immune to the sign ambiguity of ![][image8].  
2. **Phase Function (![][image21]):** This term dictates the visibility of the transmission. It acts as a specialized specular-like lobe projecting from the opposite side of the light source directly into the camera. It requires absolutely no normal data.  
3. **Energy Plausibility:** The combination mimics a Henyey-Greenstein forward-scattering lobe. By retaining the geometric attenuation, it ensures that edge-on leaves do not magically transmit light they are physically unable to intercept, preventing the foliage from glowing uniformly when side-lit.

## **4\. The Theoretical Limits of Global Sign Optimization on Vegetation**

A proposed alternative to altering the runtime shading equations to be sign-agnostic is to fix the normals offline, utilizing geometric processing to drive the neighbor sign-opposition below a 5% threshold. An extensive review of the literature on point cloud normal orientation proves this to be mathematically and practically unachievable for millions of unorganized foliage splats.

### **4.1 Propagation, Minimum Spanning Trees (MST), and Dipoles**

Early orientation algorithms, pioneered by Hoppe, attempt to orient normals by defining a seed point and propagating the normal vector across the surface via a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) weighted by Euclidean proximity32. Modern variants, such as Dipole propagation, operate on similar proximity networks.  
However, the literature explicitly notes that these methods "struggle with sparse points and tubular structures"34. The fundamental flaw is that MST propagation relies on Euclidean distance in 3D space. In a tree canopy, the nearest Euclidean neighbor to a point on a specific leaf is frequently a point on a completely separate, adjacent leaf. Propagating a normal orientation across this spatial gap results in catastrophic orientation failures. As researchers note, "faithful normals respecting sharp features tend to cause incorrect orientation propagation, and propagation orientation just using one source is problematic"36.

### **4.2 Generalized Winding Numbers (GCNO) and iPSR**

The current state-of-the-art in global orientation relies on computing the Generalized Winding Number (GWN) of the point cloud. Methods like Globally Consistent Normal Orientation (GCNO) optimize a scalar field where "the winding number is either 0 or 1" to differentiate the volumetric inside and outside of an object, subsequently aligning the normals to point outward37. Iterative Poisson Surface Reconstruction (iPSR) operates on similar volumetric implicit principles39.  
However, the literature explicitly outlines the catastrophic failure modes of these volume-based approaches when applied to vegetation. Winding numbers strictly require a closed, orientable, two-sided manifold surface20. For open boundaries, non-manifold configurations, or thin-plate (sheet-like) structures like leaves, "the notion of a global sign becomes ambiguous or ill-posed"20.

| Orientation Algorithm | Primary Mechanism | Failure Mode on Vegetation / Thin Geometry |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Hoppe MST** | Euclidean proximity propagation | Jumps between disjoint leaves; catastrophic cascading sign errors. |
| **Dipole** | Dipole field optimization | Fails on sparse tubular structures (branches) and separated point clusters. |
| **iPSR** | Iterative Poisson reconstruction | Encounters severe difficulties with sparse inputs, generating disconnected/hallucinated components40. |
| **GCNO / WNNC** | Winding number field regularization | Sign is mathematically ill-posed on thin sheets; creates "spurious closures" and "inflated thickness"20. |

When applied to thin sheets, SDFs and winding numbers attempt to artificially resolve the topological ambiguity by generating artifacts such as "spurious closures"20. The optimization routines attempt to bloat the leaf into a solid volume to satisfy the requirement that the winding number equals 1 on the interior.

### **4.3 Learned Orientations and Ray Cues**

Recent deep learning approaches, such as Hybrid Angular and Euclidean (HAE) distance encoding, attempt to resolve orientations by leveraging explicit light-ray features and LiDAR sensor origins34. While effective for terrestrial scanning where the laser origin provides a definitive "outside" vector, 3DGS inverse rendering pipelines rely on passive photogrammetry. The multi-view nature of 3DGS means that cameras observe the foliage from 360 degrees, creating highly complex, overlapping visibility fields. This prevents straightforward ray-feature fusion from establishing a consistent outward orientation for internal canopy leaves.  
**Verdict on Global Sign Optimization:** Reducing sign-opposition to \<5% on 2.4M vegetation splats is impossible. Foliage is a topological non-manifold characterized by arbitrary intersections, extreme volumetric sparsity, and no defined interior volume. The mathematical assumptions underpinning MST, Graph-Cut, and GCNO inherently collapse under these conditions. Attempting to globally optimize the point cloud will result in endless parameter tweaking, infinite optimization loops, and the hallucination of false geometry. The architectural focus must strictly remain on rendering the sign irrelevant at runtime.

## **5\. Per-Splat Normal-Confidence Measures**

While sign-agnostic shading beautifully resolves the visual artifacts of flipped normals, a secondary issue arises: certain lighting calculations (particularly sharp specular highlights and precise geometric attenuation) degrade if the underlying Gaussian primitive is highly spherical (isotropic) rather than flat (anisotropic).  
A spherical splat does not represent a local tangent plane; therefore, any normal vector extracted from it (even via the shortest axis) is mathematically arbitrary. If the runtime shader treats this arbitrary normal as a hard surface vector, it will produce unstable, flickering specular noise as the camera moves. Gating the complex PBR shading model based on a per-splat "normal-confidence" metric prevents unstable Gaussians from generating high-frequency artifacts.

### **5.1 Covariance Anisotropy as a Confidence Metric**

The geometry of a 3D Gaussian primitive is defined by its covariance matrix ![][image22], which is parameterized by a scaling vector ![][image23] and a rotation quaternion ![][image24]41. The normal is conventionally extracted by rotating the Z-axis vector \[0,0,1\] by the quaternion ![][image24], assuming ![][image25] is constrained to be the shortest axis.  
The physical reliability of this normal is directly proportional to the scale ratio between the shortest axis and the two longer axes. Recent advancements in Adaptive Anisotropic Textured Gaussians (A2TG) establish that allocating computational resources based on "the anisotropic nature of Gaussian splats" significantly improves detail preservation and efficiency42.

* A highly anisotropic splat (![][image26]) physically behaves like a 2D surfel. It accurately represents a distinct flat surface (like a leaf blade), offering absolute confidence in its normal direction.  
* Conversely, a highly isotropic splat (![][image27]) represents volumetric filler, fog, or unresolvable high-frequency noise. Its normal direction is meaningless.

**Proposed Normal Confidence (![][image28]) Formula:**  
**![][image29]**  
This formulation yields ![][image30] for perfectly flat surfels and ![][image31] for perfect spheres.

### **5.2 Visibility Agreement and Radiometric Consistency**

Advanced frameworks like Gaussian Splatting Anisotropic Visibility Field (GAVIS) and Radiometrically Consistent Gaussian Surfels (RadioGS) quantify geometric uncertainty by examining multi-view visibility agreement8. Regions with low multi-view agreement exhibit high geometric uncertainty and poor normal estimation43.  
However, evaluating visibility fields or radiometric residuals at runtime requires tracking view-dependent properties or executing expensive spherical harmonic queries during the shading pass43. For a highly performant real-time compute shader processing 2.4M splats, static geometric properties are vastly superior. Covariance anisotropy requires zero additional memory overhead and minimal ALU cost, as the scale vector ![][image32] is already fetched from VRAM during the initial rasterization and sorting phase.

### **5.3 Blending the Shading Models**

Using the calculated ![][image28] metric, the runtime shader can dynamically gate the complex PBR evaluation.

* For splats with high confidence (![][image33]), the full sign-agnostic diffuse, specular, and transmission lobes are evaluated using the explicitly derived normal.  
* For splats with low confidence (![][image34]), the shader falls back to a uniform, view-independent spherical scattering model (relying solely on the albedo and ambient lighting), preventing high-frequency specular noise from flickering on blobby, volumetric primitives.

The final radiance is a linear interpolation between the two states:  
![][image35]

## **6\. Synthesis and Implementation Deliverables**

Based on the preceding analysis, the following technical specifications are provided for direct integration into the DECISIONS D7 architecture and the M3 transmission spec.

### **6.1 Verdict on Global Sign Optimization**

**Decision: Abandon.** Pursuing global sign-consistency on dense vegetation splat clouds is mathematically intractable. Foliage geometry inherently violates the closed-manifold topology prerequisites required by advanced winding-number and Poisson reconstruction algorithms20. Resources allocated to offline geometric optimization must be diverted entirely to building a robust, sign-agnostic runtime compute shader.

### **6.2 Recommended Runtime Shading Formulation**

To eliminate the patchy shadow artifacts caused by ![][image7], all shading vectors evaluating the surface normal must be wrapped in an absolute value function.  
**Sign-Agnostic Diffuse Lobe:**

High-level shader language  
float NdotL \= dot(N, L);  
float diffuse\_intensity \= abs(NdotL);  
float3 diffuse\_color \= albedo \* diffuse\_intensity / PI;

**Sign-Agnostic Specular Lobe (Two-Sided Glossy):**

High-level shader language  
float3 H \= normalize(V \+ L);  
float NdotH \= abs(dot(N, H));  
// Evaluate microfacet NDF using the absolute half-vector dot product  
float specular\_intensity \= GGX\_NDF(NdotH, roughness);

### **6.3 Recommended Backlit / Transmission Formulation (M3 Spec)**

The previously planned transmission term—pow(max(dot(-N,L),0)\*0.5+0.5, wrap\_power)—must be discarded. Under sign-agnostic operations, ![][image9] evaluates identically to ![][image8]. Transmission must be entirely decoupled from the normal's direction and tied instead to the phase angle between the View (![][image16]) and Light (![][image17]) vectors. The absolute normal is used strictly for geometric surface-area attenuation.  
**Phase-Driven Transmission Lobe:**

High-level shader language  
// Assuming V and L point outward away from the surface  
float phase\_angle \= dot(V, \-L);   
float wrap\_phase \= pow(max(phase\_angle, 0.0) \* 0.5 \+ 0.5, wrap\_power);  
float geometric\_attenuation \= abs(dot(N, L));

// The final transmission relies on recovered transmission\_albedo  
float3 transmission\_color \= transmission\_albedo \* geometric\_attenuation \* wrap\_phase;

### **6.4 Normal Confidence Implementation**

To prevent shading artifacts on poorly reconstructed, isotropic Gaussians, implement the following gating metric based on the primitive's scale vector.

High-level shader language  
// Calculate anisotropy confidence based on the scale vector  
float min\_scale \= min(scale.x, min(scale.y, scale.z));  
float max\_scale \= max(scale.x, max(scale.y, scale.z));  
float mid\_scale \= (scale.x \+ scale.y \+ scale.z) \- min\_scale \- max\_scale;

float mean\_major\_scale \= (mid\_scale \+ max\_scale) \* 0.5;  
float C\_N \= saturate(1.0 \- (min\_scale / (mean\_major\_scale \+ 1e-6)));

// Fall back to a simple view-independent ambient term for blobby splats  
float3 final\_shading \= lerp(volumetric\_ambient\_lighting, sign\_agnostic\_pbr\_lighting, C\_N);

By adopting these formulations, the D7 rendering pipeline will become mathematically immune to the \~30% sign-inversion floor. This ensures temporally stable, photorealistic relighting of complex foliage, preserving high-frequency detail without relying on computationally prohibitive and theoretically flawed topological constraints.

#### **Works cited**

1. GIR: 3D Gaussian Inverse Rendering for Relightable Scene Factorization, [https://3dgir.github.io/](https://3dgir.github.io/)  
2. GIR: 3D Gaussian Inverse Rendering for Relightable Scene Factorization \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2312.05133v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2312.05133v1)  
3. \[2312.05133\] GIR: 3D Gaussian Inverse Rendering for Relightable Scene Factorization, [https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.05133](https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.05133)  
4. CVPR Poster GaussianShader: 3D Gaussian Splatting with Shading Functions for Reflective Surfaces, [https://cvpr.thecvf.com/virtual/2024/poster/30324](https://cvpr.thecvf.com/virtual/2024/poster/30324)  
5. GaussianShader: 3D Gaussian Splatting with Shading Functions for Reflective Surfaces \- CVF Open Access, [https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024/papers/Jiang\_GaussianShader\_3D\_Gaussian\_Splatting\_with\_Shading\_Functions\_for\_Reflective\_Surfaces\_CVPR\_2024\_paper.pdf](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024/papers/Jiang_GaussianShader_3D_Gaussian_Splatting_with_Shading_Functions_for_Reflective_Surfaces_CVPR_2024_paper.pdf)  
6. GaussianShader: 3D Gaussian Splatting with Shading Functions for Reflective Surfaces \- CVPR 2024 Open Access Repository \- The Computer Vision Foundation, [https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024/html/Jiang\_GaussianShader\_3D\_Gaussian\_Splatting\_with\_Shading\_Functions\_for\_Reflective\_Surfaces\_CVPR\_2024\_paper.html](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024/html/Jiang_GaussianShader_3D_Gaussian_Splatting_with_Shading_Functions_for_Reflective_Surfaces_CVPR_2024_paper.html)  
7. IRGS: Inter-Reflective Gaussian Splatting with 2D Gaussian Ray Tracing \- CVF Open Access, [https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2025/papers/Gu\_IRGS\_Inter-Reflective\_Gaussian\_Splatting\_with\_2D\_Gaussian\_Ray\_Tracing\_CVPR\_2025\_paper.pdf](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2025/papers/Gu_IRGS_Inter-Reflective_Gaussian_Splatting_with_2D_Gaussian_Ray_Tracing_CVPR_2025_paper.pdf)  
8. Radiometrically Consistent Gaussian Surfels for Inverse Rendering \- OpenReview, [https://openreview.net/forum?id=lKqE7UuMvp](https://openreview.net/forum?id=lKqE7UuMvp)  
9. GS-IR: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Inverse Rendering \- CVF Open Access, [https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024/papers/Liang\_GS-IR\_3D\_Gaussian\_Splatting\_for\_Inverse\_Rendering\_CVPR\_2024\_paper.pdf](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024/papers/Liang_GS-IR_3D_Gaussian_Splatting_for_Inverse_Rendering_CVPR_2024_paper.pdf)

11. \[2311.16473\] GS-IR: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Inverse Rendering \- ar5iv \- arXiv, [https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2311.16473](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2311.16473)  
12. GS-IR: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Inverse Rendering \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2311.16473v3](https://arxiv.org/html/2311.16473v3)  
13. DeferredGS: Decoupled and Relightable Gaussian Splatting With Deferred Shading \- IEEE Computer Society, [https://www.computer.org/csdl/journal/tp/2025/08/10964878/25UA9X1ewco](https://www.computer.org/csdl/journal/tp/2025/08/10964878/25UA9X1ewco)  
14. DeferredGS: Decoupled and Editable Gaussian Splatting with Deferred Shading \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2404.09412v2](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.09412v2)  
15. GS-ID: Illumination Decomposition on Gaussian Splatting via Diffusion Prior and Parametric Light Source Optimization \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2408.08524v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2408.08524v1)  
16. GS-ID: Illumination Decomposition on Gaussian Splatting, [https://kangdu.top/gsid/](https://kangdu.top/gsid/)  
17. Illumination Decomposition on Gaussian Splatting via Adaptive Light Aggregation and Diffusion-Guided Material Priors \- hkust (gz), [https://cislab.hkust-gz.edu.cn/media/documents/GS-ID.pdf](https://cislab.hkust-gz.edu.cn/media/documents/GS-ID.pdf)  
18. Relightable 3D Gaussian \- NJU-3DV, [https://nju-3dv.github.io/projects/Relightable3DGaussian/](https://nju-3dv.github.io/projects/Relightable3DGaussian/)  
19. Relightable 3D Gaussians: Realistic Point Cloud Relighting with BRDF Decomposition and Ray Tracing \- ECVA, [https://www.ecva.net/papers/eccv\_2024/papers\_ECCV/papers/06121.pdf](https://www.ecva.net/papers/eccv_2024/papers_ECCV/papers/06121.pdf)  
20. Metric–-Phase Fields: Decoupling Distance and Sign for Thin-Structure Reconstruction from Unoriented Point Clouds \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2605.25503v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.25503v1)  
21. Unreal Engine Material Properties \- Epic Games Developers, [https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/unreal-engine-material-properties](https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/unreal-engine-material-properties)  
22. Shading Models in Unreal Engine \- Epic Games Developers, [https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/shading-models-in-unreal-engine](https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/shading-models-in-unreal-engine)

24. How to make the landscape and grass the same color? They are the same in texture editor. How to make that lighting affects them the same way? : r/unrealengine \- Reddit, [https://www.reddit.com/r/unrealengine/comments/jzgrtj/how\_to\_make\_the\_landscape\_and\_grass\_the\_same/](https://www.reddit.com/r/unrealengine/comments/jzgrtj/how_to_make_the_landscape_and_grass_the_same/)
